title: List of New Scientist - Home articles
author: adminadmin

items.title: Frailty sets in far earlier than you’d expect, but you can reverse it

content.content: We’re learning that frailty can quietly arrive decades before old age, with some people in their 30s or 40s unknowingly in a pre-frail state. There are surprising ways to stay strong – and it’s not all about weight training

items.title: We’ve only just confirmed that Homo habilis really existed

content.content: Their species name is well known, but until recently we’ve understood very little for certain about Homo habilis. Columnist Michael Marshall reveals what new fossils are telling us about the hominins that have been considered the first humans

items.title: Mathematics is undergoing the biggest change in its history

content.content: The speed at which artificial intelligence is gaining in mathematical ability has taken many by surprise. It is rewriting what it means to be a mathematician

items.title: How our ancestors used mushrooms to change the course of human history

content.content: Mushrooms have been used by ancient humans for millennia, but archaeologists have only just uncovered their pivotal role in shaping civilisation

items.title: Parkinson's disease may reduce enjoyment of pleasant smells

content.content: The "world smells different" for people with Parkinson's disease, a discovery that could help doctors spot the condition sooner

items.title: King penguins are thriving in a warmer climate, but it may not last

content.content: Longer summers are allowing more king penguin chicks to bulk up and survive the winter, but the penguins' main fishing area is shifting further away as temperatures rise

items.title: A miniature magnet rivals behemoths in strength for the first time

content.content: Strong magnets tend to be large and power-hungry, but a new design has produced a powerful magnet that fits in the palm of your hand, making it more practical and affordable

items.title: Undisclosed ads on TikTok skirt ban on profiling minors

content.content: Teenagers are being bombarded with highly targeted commercial content on TikTok, despite an EU law that prohibits profiling minors for advertising

items.title: Rumours of a Firefly reboot abound, but should the Serenity fly again?

content.content: Star Nathan Fillion is stoking rumours that cult western-in-space television series Firefly could be rebooted. Emily H. Wilson realises she is being toyed with – but is still praying for its return

items.title: Why drug overdose deaths have suddenly plummeted in the US

content.content: Fentanyl-related overdose deaths fell by nearly 30 per cent in the space of a year in the US, which could represent a significant turning point in the country's opioid addiction crisis

items.title: Chemistry may not be the 'killer app' for quantum computers after all

content.content: Two popular quantum computing algorithms for problems in chemistry may have very limited use even as quantum hardware improves

items.title: Can species evolve fast enough to survive as the planet heats up?

content.content: The story of a wildflower that adapted to a severe drought in California raises hopes that evolution will come to the rescue of species hit by climate change, but there are limits

items.title: How worried should you be about your BMI?

content.content: Body mass index (BMI) is used as a global standard for measuring health, but does it actually tell you anything about how healthy you are on an individual level? Carissa Wong explains the problems with this flawed tool

items.title: We don’t know if AI-powered toys are safe, but they’re here anyway

content.content: Toys powered by AI show a worrying lack of emotional understanding. But we need to understand the risks and benefits of the technology so the industry can be regulated, not outright banned

items.title: The race to solve the biggest problem in quantum computing

content.content: The errors that quantum computers make are holding the technology back. But recent progress in quantum error correction has excited many researchers

items.title: A glimpse into the rare earth riches of Greenland

content.content: Photographer Jonas Kako travelled to Greenland to explore how mining for the rare earth elements and minerals that are vital for new green technologies is impacting locals

items.title: New Scientist recommends sci-fi novel Under the Eye of the Big Bird

content.content: The books, TV, games and more that New Scientist staff have enjoyed this week

items.title: Why are we so suspicious of do-gooders?

content.content: A growing body of research shows that we tend to discount a person’s good deeds if they stand to benefit from them. Columnist David Robson explores where this instinct comes from – and whether we should resist it

items.title: The 3 things you need to know about passwords, from a security expert

content.content: There are a few simple things you can do to make your digital life much more secure, says cybersecurity expert Jake Moore - follow these tips to tighten up your passwords

items.title: Single-celled organism with no brain is capable of Pavlovian learning

content.content: A trumpet-shaped, single-celled organism seems able to predict one thing will follow another, hinting that such associative learning emerged long before multicellular nervous systems

items.title: Our extinct Australopithecus relatives may have had difficult births

content.content: Simulations of Australopithecus hominins’ anatomy suggest that when they gave birth, they may have exerted tremendous pressure on their pelvic floors, putting them at risk of tearing

items.title: A smartphone app can help men last longer in bed

content.content: In a randomised trial, men who experience premature ejaculation benefitted from using an app to learn techniques for extending intercourse

items.title: Why are we so obsessed with protein? A new book looks for answers

content.content: Samantha King and Gavin Weedon's new book Protein digs deep into the nutrient's role in our health. But can it tell you how much you should be eating? Alexandra Thompson explores

items.title: How a midlife tune-up could help prepare you for a healthy old age

content.content: Most of us don’t worry about our health in old age until we get there, but research is increasingly showing that how you live in your mid-50s can have a real impact in your 90s

items.title: What does it mean if the universe has extra dimensions?

content.content: Dimensions beyond the four we’re familiar with could solve a host of problems in physics and cosmology. Columnist Leah Crane explores what a higher-dimensional universe might be like – and how we could find out if we live in one

items.title: The ancient Goths were an ethnically diverse group

content.content: Ancient DNA reveals that the Goths of eastern Europe, some of whom would ultimately sack the city of Rome, may have been a mix of peoples from three continents

items.title: AI is nearly exclusively designed by men – here's how to fix it

content.content: With the Trump administration’s attacks on so-called woke AI it is becoming even harder to make the technology we use fairer and more diverse. Leading voices are speaking out, reports Catherine de Lange

items.title: Why global warming is accelerating and what it means for the future

content.content: Scientists disagree whether human-made climate change or natural fluctuations are mostly to blame for worse-than-expected heat in recent years

items.title: The asteroid Ryugu has all of the main ingredients for life

content.content: All five of the canonical nucleobases – the underpinnings of DNA, RNA and life on Earth – have been found in samples from the asteroid Ryugu

items.title: Forget the multiverse. In the pluriverse, we create reality together

content.content: A radical idea that resolves many quantum paradoxes suggests there is no objective view of reality. How can the cosmos be stitched together from interlocking perspectives?

items.title: 3I/ATLAS: Interstellar comet has water unlike any in our solar system

content.content: The levels of a heavy form of hydrogen in 3I/ATLAS are 30 to 40 times higher than in Earth's oceans, suggesting the comet has a cold and distant origin

items.title: Particle discovered at CERN solves a 20-year-old mystery

content.content: Physicists working on the LHCb experiment have spotted an elusive and fleeting particle, a heavier and more charming cousin to the proton, that has been sought for decades

items.title: A very serious guide to buying your own humanoid robot butler

content.content: You can now buy a humanoid robot housekeeper for less than the price of a second-hand car. But before splashing out, there’s something you need to know

items.title: Social media is a defective product

content.content: Two lawsuits are being brought against giant tech firms for the dangers their apps pose to young people. Columnist Annalee Newitz says the outcome of those cases could dramatically change social media for the better

items.title: Psychedelics may be no better than antidepressants for depression

content.content: Drugs like psilocybin that induce psychedelic effects have shown promise for treating depression. Now, a review of the evidence suggests that they are effective, but no more so than traditional antidepressants

items.title: Ice core reveals low CO2 during warm spell 3 million years ago

content.content: For the first time, scientists have measured atmospheric gases from the late Pliocene, yielding data that could help to predict the future climate

items.title: The mystery of how volcanic lightning happens has been solved

content.content: When particles in volcanic ash cloud rub together, some pick up positive charge and others negative – now physicists have finally elucidated how these different charges are determined

items.title: Will war in the Middle East accelerate the clean energy transition?

content.content: Disruption to shipping traffic through the Strait of Hormuz has led to a spike in oil and natural gas prices, which could spur countries to boost the roll-out of renewable energy and electric vehicles

items.title: Neanderthals may have treated wounds with antibiotic sticky tar

content.content: Tar made from birch tree bark is commonly found at Neanderthal sites, and experiments show that it kills some bacteria that cause skin infections

items.title: Boosting the blood-brain barrier could avert brain damage in athletes

content.content: The neurodegenerative condition chronic traumatic encephalopathy appears to be driven by damage to the blood-brain barrier due to repetitive head injuries, like those that occur in boxing. This suggests that drugs that strengthen this barrier could prevent or slow the condition

items.title: Fluorescent ruby-like gems have been found on Mars for the first time

content.content: The Perseverance rover has found tiny crystals that seem to be rubies or sapphires inside pebbles on Mars, where they have never been seen before

items.title: Maggie Aderin's dream: To walk by the footprints of Neil Armstrong

content.content: Space scientist Maggie Aderin talks telescopes, neurodiversity and being underestimated with Rowan Hooper on the New Scientist podcast, as her memoir Starchild comes out

items.title: It's time to monetise the moon! Definitely! Maybe?

content.content: Feedback discovers an accounting firm has unveiled its latest "lunar market assessment", which predicts huge profits to be had. Suit up, lunar entrepreneurs!

items.title: How worried should you be about ultra-processed foods?

content.content: We are constantly told to watch out for the health risks of eating ultra-processed food, but should you be worried every time you sit down for a meal? Sam Wong takes a look at the evidence

items.title: Physicists create formula for how many times you can fold a crêpe

content.content: When you fold a flexible material such as a pancake or a tortilla, its behaviour depends on a competition between gravity and elasticity

items.title: Mathematician wins 2026 Abel prize for solving 60-year-old mystery

content.content: Gerd Faltings shocked mathematicians around the world for his 1983 proof of the Mordell conjecture, which brought together seemingly disparate mathematical fields

items.title: Probiotic cream that ramps up heat production could prevent frostbite

content.content: Tweaking our skin's microbiome via a probiotic cream could prevent frostbite and hypothermia in extreme environments

items.title: Rebecca Solnit: 'The great majority of people want climate action'

content.content: Climate activist and author Rebecca Solnit tells Rowan Hooper why she still has hope, even in these "catastrophic" times

items.title: Your partner probably wakes you up at night without you even realising

content.content: People who share a bed with a partner are woken by them multiple times per night, but don’t remember most of these disturbances

items.title: Route-planning AI cut climate-warming contrails on over 100 flights

content.content: A weather-forecasting AI was used to recommend routes for American Airlines flights between the US and Europe to reduce the formation of contrails, which contribute to global warming
